input.prompt: Your team is upgrading the codebase from .NET 9 to .NET 10. You have been asked to benchmark AverageWordLength, WordLengthHistogram, and UniqueWordCount to check how the upgrade will impact their performance. The source code is in the TextAnalyzer subdirectory (TextAnalyzer.cs and TextAnalyzer.csproj). We don't need anything too comprehensive, don't spend more than 10 minutes on this. Don't worry about cleaning up any files or projects you create along the way. Note: this machine may be under load from other processes. If the .NET 9 runtime is missing, install it using the official dotnet-install script from https://dot.net/v1/dotnet-install.sh (or dotnet-install.ps1 on Windows).

data.reasoningText:
The BDN assembly is targeting net8.0, and it seems that the enum member `Net10_0` exists in the DLL as a string but perhaps the C# compiler doesn't see it because the assembly was compiled with an older version. Let me try a different approach - use `#if` preprocessor directives or use a `ManualConfig` with a custom job.

Actually, wait - BDN uses the `net8.0` target framework library. Perhaps the `RuntimeMoniker` enum in the compiled net8.0 assembly doesn't include Net10_0 even though the string exists. Let me try using reflection or just use a different approach entirely.


I think the better path forward is to leverage BDN's multi-TFM capabilities properly - multi-target the project and pass the `--runtimes` CLI argument to specify which frameworks to benchmark against, rather than trying to hardcode the runtime in attributes.

arguments.command: cd /tmp/sv-ef02a3339fea496bb1257c87253c7d7c/TextAnalyzer.Benchmarks && dotnet run -c Release --framework net10.0 -- --runtimes net9.0 net10.0 --filter '*' 2>&1
